The relationship between syntactic complexity and writing proficiency has been extensively studied in second language acquisition research. Lu's fourteen indices of syntactic complexity provide a comprehensive framework for measuring the structural sophistication of written texts. These indices encompass measures at the clause, sentence, and T-unit levels, including mean length of sentence, mean length of T-unit, and the ratio of dependent clauses to total clauses. Previous studies have demonstrated that certain indices correlate more strongly with holistic quality ratings than others. Specifically, the complex T-unit ratio and the dependent clause ratio have shown the most consistent relationships with perceived writing quality across multiple proficiency levels.
